chapter_summary: After crossing the ocean with Vinata to inspect the divine horse, Kadru wins the wager: seeing the black hairs on the horse's tail, she binds Vinata into servitude, and the defeated Vinata sinks into sorrow and enters the state of a slave (dasya). At that same appointed time, Garuda is born — bursting his egg unaided by his mother, a being of immense splendor. Blazing like a heap of fire and terrifying, he suddenly swells to a colossal body and rises into the sky. All creatures, mistaking his blaze for a world-ending fire, flee for refuge to Agni (Vibhavasu), bowing to him in his universal form and begging him not to burn them. Agni corrects them: this is not me but Garuda, mighty and equal to me in radiance. The gods together with the hosts of sages then approach Garuda and praise him with hymns — hailing him as rishi, god, lord of birds, sun-like lord, refuge, and the very fire of cosmic dissolution. Praised thus, Suparna (Garuda) withdraws his own blazing energy.

story.title: The Birth of Garuda and the Enslavement of Vinata
story.arc: Kadru wins the cruel wager over the colour of the divine horse's tail and binds her co-wife Vinata into servitude. At that very hour Vinata's son Garuda bursts from his egg, fully formed and blazing, swelling into a cosmic body that terrifies all creation. They flee to Agni thinking the world is ending; Agni reveals the blaze is Garuda, his equal. Gods and sages then hymn the newborn bird-lord, and Garuda graciously withdraws his fire.
story.timeline_note: Vinata's enslavement and Garuda's birth occur simultaneously ('at the appointed time'); this chapter immediately precedes Garuda's exploits to free his mother.
story.comic_potential: The entire pantheon stampeding in panic — convinced doomsday has arrived — only for Agni to sheepishly clarify 'relax, that's just a newborn bird, he's about my size.' The cosmic over-reaction to an infant is pure visual comedy.

teachings.teaching: A bond entered through a rigged or deceitful wager (Kadru's tail trick) binds the defeated even when the cause is unjust — Vinata still falls into servitude.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya framework, bondage (dasya) is the soul's entanglement in conditioned outcomes; one is enslaved not by external decree but by having staked oneself on the perishable. Liberation begins when the inner Garuda — pure ascending power — is born.

teachings.teaching: The supreme power is mistaken for destruction by the unawakened; creatures flee Garuda's blaze as if it were the fire of dissolution, unable to tell the radiance of a divine being from annihilation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma reads the kundalini's awakening as a 'heap of fire' (agnirasi) rising — terrifying to the ego, which experiences the ascent of consciousness as a world-ending blaze until it learns the fire is its own liberating Self, not its destroyer.

teachings.teaching: A realised being voluntarily withholds its full power out of compassion; praised by gods and sages, Garuda draws his blazing energy back into himself rather than consume the world.
teachings.lens_note: For Sharma the master of the awakened fire controls its withdrawal (pratisamhara) — the yogi who has raised the energy can re-absorb it at will, the mark of mastery being restraint, not display.
